items.title: Edinburgh and Glasgow Union Canal Company Act 1817
items.summary: Local Act incorporating the Edinburgh and Glasgow Union Canal Company, granting powers to construct and operate a canal linking Edinburgh and Glasgow.
items.reason: This 1817 act is obsolete; the canal exists today under modern management by public trusts and navigation authorities, with governance superseded by subsequent legislation like the Transport Act 1968 and canal nationalization. Keeping it perpetuates statutory clutter, risks unintended legal ambiguities (e.g., dormant company powers or property claims), distorts incentives by preserving outdated privileges, and offers no ongoing benefits to prosperity or liberty as private enterprise has evolved beyond such specific grants.

items.title: Belfast Charitable Society and Water Supply Act 1817

Keeping it burdens the statute book with archaic provisions, fostering legal uncertainty, compliance costs, and potential conflicts with contemporary laws, while distorting incentives without delivering net benefits—private property and market institutions would handle water provision more efficiently today.

items.title: Blackfriars Bridge (Manchester) Act 1817
items.summary: Local Act from 1817 authorizing or regulating the construction, maintenance, or operation of Blackfriars Bridge in Manchester.

items.title: Great Bolton Improvement Act 1817
items.summary: Local Act for the improvement of Great Bolton (now part of Bolton), empowering commissioners to pave and repair streets, provide lighting and sewerage, regulate markets and nuisances, levy local rates on properties, and manage town infrastructure.
items.reason: This 1817 Act imposes outdated local taxes (rates) and regulatory powers on unelected commissioners, creating distortions in property rights, potential monopolies in services like lighting and markets, and administrative burdens long superseded by modern local government acts (e.g., Public Health Act 1875, Local Government Acts). Keeping it clutters the statute book, risks legal conflicts with current planning and highways laws, stifles property development incentives, and yields no net benefits as private enterprise and elected councils now handle such functions more efficiently; unseen costs include reduced local entrepreneurship and innovation in urban services.

items.title: Shrewsbury and Bridgnorth Road Act 1817
items.summary: Local Act authorizing the establishment, construction, maintenance, and toll collection for a turnpike road between Shrewsbury and Bridgnorth in Shropshire.
items.reason: This 1817 turnpike road act is obsolete, as modern UK roads are managed under comprehensive highways legislation (e.g., Highways Act 1980). Retaining it clogs the statute book, imposes unseen costs on legal research and compliance, risks conflicts with current property rights and infrastructure management, and distorts incentives by preserving archaic private toll privileges that no longer function, hindering clear modern institutions for transport.

items.title: City of London Gauger Act 1817
items.summary: Local Act regulating gaugers (officials measuring cask capacities for duties or trade standards) in the City of London, enacted in 1817.
items.reason: This 200+ year-old local regulation imposes outdated government oversight on private trade measurements, likely superseded by national weights and measures laws; retains bureaucratic costs, potential for rent-seeking by officials, distorts market incentives for accurate voluntary standards, and clogs the legal system with obsolete rules preventing clearer modern institutions for commerce.

items.title: British Gallery of Pictures Act 1817
items.summary: The British Gallery of Pictures Act 1817 (c. lxi) is a local UK Act from the reign of George III, likely establishing or regulating a public gallery for displaying British paintings, involving government authorization, potential public funding, or property rights for an art institution.
items.reason: This 19th-century act exemplifies government intervention in cultural affairs, diverting taxpayer resources or imposing restrictions on private property for a non-essential public gallery. Private philanthropy, markets, and voluntary associations can amply support art institutions without state compulsion, as seen globally. Retaining it perpetuates legislative bloat, administrative costs, and distorted incentives in the arts market (e.g., favoring state-approved works, crowding out private galleries), with unseen consequences like opportunity costs for more productive uses of capital. No evidence it uniquely achieves prosperity-enhancing outcomes unobtainable privately; deletion frees resources and simplifies law without harming citizens.

items.title: Commercial Docks Act 1817
items.summary: The Commercial Docks Act 1817 is a local UK Act (c. lxii) that authorized the construction, management, and operation of commercial docks, likely in the London area (e.g., Surrey Commercial Docks), granting specific powers to proprietors including levying dues, compulsory land acquisition, and regulatory privileges for dock operations.
items.reason: This 19th-century local act exemplifies government-granted monopolies and eminent domain for private infrastructure, distorting markets by shielding operators from competition, violating property rights through forced acquisitions, and imposing tolls that raise costs without market discipline. Now obsolete with docks long redeveloped or superseded by modern ports, retaining it clutters the legal system, creates uncertainty for property owners, and blocks efficient land use; unseen costs include foregone redevelopment opportunities and perpetuation of outdated privileges that hinder entrepreneurship and wealth creation via free markets and voluntary contracts.

items.reason: This 1817 local act is obsolete, regulating 19th-century waterways superseded by modern transport infrastructure like roads, rail, and ports. Deletion eliminates unseen compliance costs and bureaucratic remnants without harming navigation or prosperity.

items.title: Taunton Roads Act 1817

Deleting it imposes no costs—roads in Taunton function fine without it—and aligns with institutional simplification to foster prosperity by reducing regulatory deadweight.

items.summary: Local Act (1817 c. lxxvi) establishing or incorporating the Royal Naval Asylum, an institution for the maintenance and education of children of deceased Royal Navy personnel.
items.reason: This 200+ year-old act is obsolete, with its functions superseded by modern private charities, military welfare provisions, and state benefits that operate without such rigid statutory frameworks. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: administrative burden on legal systems, potential for archaic clauses to conflict with current laws, distortion of incentives for private philanthropy by implying ongoing state preference for a defunct entity, and reduced legal clarity that hampers efficient property and institutional evolution.

items.title: East India Company and the Nabobs of the Carnatic Act 1817
items.summary: Local Act from 1817 addressing financial arrangements, likely debts or settlements, between the East India Company and the Nabobs of the Carnatic in colonial India.
items.reason: This 200-year-old legislation concerns defunct entities (East India Company dissolved 1874) and resolved historical colonial debts with no modern application or enforcement. Keeping it imposes unseen costs via legal bloat: complicates statute book navigation, raises expenses for legal research/compliance, and diverts resources from active laws, distorting institutional efficiency without benefiting liberty, property, or prosperity.

items.title: Northampton Judges' Accommodations Act 1817
items.summary: Northampton Judges' Accommodations Act 1817 (local act, 57 Geo 3 c ix), providing for accommodations for judges in Northampton.
items.reason: This 1817 local act is obsolete, addressing a specific historical need for judges' lodging in one town that is now handled through modern general budgets, private hotels, and market mechanisms. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: legal clutter confusing citizens and lawyers, administrative burdens on archives, and potential for archaic provisions to distort incentives or be misused. Deletion promotes clarity and liberty without harming judicial functions or prosperity.

items.title: Deserted Tenements Act 1817 (repealed)
items.summary: 1817 Act amending 1738 legislation to allow landlords quicker repossession of rack-rent or high-rent properties abandoned by tenants in arrears for half a year's rent (down from one year), even without reserved re-entry rights, via justices' inspection if no sufficient distress available.
items.reason: This law protects landlords' private property rights by providing a swift, low-cost mechanism to repossess clearly abandoned premises after non-payment, preventing losses from uncultivated land and enabling prompt re-letting. Without it, landlords would face prolonged vacancy, higher costs, and legal delays, distorting rental markets, reducing housing supply, and eroding incentives to invest in property—making British citizens, especially property owners, worse off as secure property rights are foundational to wealth creation and liberty.

items.title: Treason Act 1817 (repealed 30.9.1998)
items.summary: The Treason Act 1817 extended and made perpetual provisions from the 1795 Treason Act, deeming it high treason (punishable by death) to compass, imagine, or intend harm to the monarch if expressed via printing, writing, or overt act, upon conviction by two witnesses or due process. It was repealed on 30 September 1998.
items.reason: Although protecting the sovereign is foundational to stable institutions enabling liberty and property rights, this Act's broad criminalization of 'imagining' or intending harm—expressed in speech or writing—creates severe chilling effects on free expression, political dissent, and individualism, with high risk of abuse by authorities to suppress opposition. Unintended consequences include distorted incentives against open debate, potential monopolies on political power, and reduced prosperity from stifled innovation in ideas; modern, narrower treason laws suffice without such overreach.

items.title: Public Offices (Scotland) Act 1817
items.summary: 1817 Act regulating salaries and fees for various Scottish public offices (e.g., capping Keeper of Great Seal at £2,000/annum, Privy Seal at £1,200), merging duties (e.g., Keeper of Signet into Lord Register), requiring personal discharge of duties, directing fee surpluses to public treasury, and abolishing redundant offices like Clerk of the Pipe and Comptroller General of Customs.
items.reason: This Act exemplifies fiscal restraint by slashing sinecures, merging roles, and redirecting surpluses to taxpayers, aligning with principles of limited government and efficient resource use. However, as a 200+ year-old relic, its specific provisions are likely obsolete—offices reformed, abolished, or superseded—creating unseen costs in legal clutter, interpretive ambiguity, and administrative overhead that distort incentives for modern governance without delivering ongoing savings or protections.

items.title: Distress (Costs) Act 1817 (repealed 8.11.1995)
items.summary: 1817 Act capping costs and charges for distress (seizure of goods) levied for rents up to £20, mandating a fee schedule, requiring brokers to provide charge copies, and allowing aggrieved parties to seek treble damages plus costs via Justice of the Peace, with witness summons powers.
items.reason: This imposes price controls on bailiff and broker services for enforcing rent payment via distress, distorting market incentives and property rights enforcement. Unintended consequences include bailiffs avoiding low-value jobs (reducing supply of services for small landlords), landlords facing higher risks of non-payment (leading to fewer rentals for low-income tenants, higher rents, or exit from market), and added bureaucracy/costs of JP hearings that burden the poor it aims to protect. Competition, reputation, and contract terms would better regulate fees without government caps, aligning with liberty and spontaneous order; modern tenancy laws offer superior protections.

items.title: Duchy of Lancaster Act 1817
items.summary: 1817 Act ratifying a specific agreement for the Crown to purchase estates near the Forest of Dean from Viscount Gage for £155,863, authorizing Commissioners of Woods, Forests, and Land Revenues to sell other Crown lands to fund it, and granting broader powers to buy/sell Crown properties for efficient management (e.g., consolidating near forests), with provisions for certificates, indemnities, leases, and specific disposals like Newmarket properties and rents.
items.reason: This archaic act perpetuates state ownership and bureaucratic management of Crown lands through politically approved buy/sell powers, distorting private property markets, enabling inefficient allocations via government discretion rather than competitive markets, and blocking full privatization that would unleash wealth creation via individual ownership and incentives. Unseen costs include foregone productivity from non-market decisions, administrative overhead, and legal clutter confusing modern estate management; British citizens would gain from deleting it to facilitate privatization and reduce state intervention in land.
